input.initialPrompt:
I'm setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11 from scratch. I need
to target both Android and Windows. I can't run commands right now — give
me a complete step-by-step plan with all the PowerShell commands I'll need,
including how to discover the right versions dynamically.

data.content:
Here's a complete plan for setting up .NET MAUI on Windows 11 (Android + Windows targets).

---

## Step 1 — Install .NET SDK

```powershell
# Download and run the official installer (latest .NET 8 or 9)
winget install Microsoft.DotNet.SDK.9
# Verify
dotnet --version
```

## Step 2 — Install the MAUI Workload

```powershell
# List available workloads to discover the exact name
dotnet workload search maui

# Install the MAUI workload (includes Android, Windows, iOS stubs)
dotnet workload install maui

# Verify it's installed
dotnet workload list
```

## Step 3 — Install Visual Studio Build Tools (Windows target requires WinUI SDK)

The Windows (WinUI 3) target needs the Windows App SDK, which comes with Visual Studio. If you want **VS Code only**, you still need the build tools:

```powershell
# Option A: Install full Visual Studio Community (recommended)
winget install Microsoft.VisualStudio.2022.Community

# Then add required workloads via the VS Installer CLI:
& "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio\Installer\vs_installer.exe" modify `
  --installPath "C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Community" `
  --add Microsoft.VisualStudio.Workload.NetCrossPlat `
  --add Microsoft.VisualStudio.Workload.ManagedDesktop `
  --passive --norestart

# Option B: Build Tools only (no IDE)
winget install Microsoft.VisualStudio.2022.BuildTools
```

## Step 4 — Android SDK Setup

The MAUI workload bundles an Android SDK, but you may need to accept licenses and verify paths:

```powershell
# Discover where the Android SDK was installed
dotnet build -t:InstallAndroidDependencies -f net9.0-android `
  -p:AndroidSdkDirectory="$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk" `
  -p:AcceptAndroidSDKLicenses=true

# If you need standalone SDK management, install cmdline-tools:
winget install Google.AndroidCommandLineTools

# Find installed SDK components
& "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --list

# Install required components dynamically
& "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" `
  "platform-tools" `
  "platforms;android-35" `
  "build-tools;35.0.0" `
  "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64"

# Accept all licenses
& "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --licenses
```

## Step 5 — Android Emulator (optional but recommended)

```powershell
# Install the emulator
& "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" "emulator"

# Create an AVD — discover available device definitions first
& "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\avdmanager.bat" list device

# Create one
& "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\avdmanager.bat" create avd `
  -n "Pixel_7_API_35" -k "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64" -d "pixel_7"

# Enable hardware acceleration (requires Hyper-V or HAXM)
Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName Microsoft-Hyper-V-All -NoRestart
```

## Step 6 — Set Environment Variables

```powershell
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("ANDROID_HOME",
  "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk", "User")
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("JAVA_HOME",
  (Get-ChildItem "$env:ProgramFiles\Microsoft\jdk-*" | Sort-Object Name -Descending | Select-Object -First 1).FullName,
  "User")

# Add platform-tools to PATH for adb
$currentPath = [System.Environment]::GetEnvironmentVariable("Path", "User")
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("Path",
  "$currentPath;$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk\platform-tools", "User")
```

## Step 7 — Enable Developer Mode (required for Windows target)

```powershell
# Enable Developer Mode for sideloading WinUI apps
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock" `
  /v AllowDevelopmentWithoutDevLicense /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f
```

## Step 8 — Create and Run a Test Project

```powershell
# Scaffold a new MAUI app
dotnet new maui -n TestMauiApp
cd TestMauiApp

# Build for Windows (WinUI 3)
dotnet build -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Run on Windows
dotnet run -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Build for Android
dotnet build -f net9.0-android

# Deploy to running emulator
dotnet run -f net9.0-android
```

## Step 9 — Discover Versions Dynamically (reference commands)

```powershell
# See all installed .NET SDKs
dotnet --list-sdks

# See all installed workloads + their versions
dotnet workload list --versions

# See available target frameworks for MAUI templates
dotnet new maui --dry-run | Select-String "framework"

# Check installed Android platforms/build-tools
& "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --list_installed

# Check Java version (MAUI bundles Microsoft OpenJDK)
& "$env:JAVA_HOME\bin\java.exe" -version
```

---

**Key gotcha**: The Windows target (`net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0`) only builds on Windows with the Windows App SDK present — that's why Visual Studio or its Build Tools are required even if you code in VS Code. The Android target works with just the .NET SDK + workload.
data.reasoningText: The user wants a complete step-by-step plan for setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11, targeting Android and Windows. They can't run commands now, so they want all the PowerShell commands laid out. Let me provide a comprehensive guide.
